critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-constructed with temporal splitting, explicit leakage controls, and a reasonable baseline. I found a small number of genuine issues worth raising, and several apparent concerns that dissolve on close inspection.

**Issue 1: The rolling 24-hour contact-intensity score is a potential label leakage vector**

The claim: structured features are all available at prediction time and therefore safe to include.

Why it may be wrong: The rolling 24-hour contact-intensity score 'summarizes support touches and routing events associated with the customer.' If this score is computed by the support platform at any point during the ticket's lifecycle rather than strictly at ticket-open time, it may incorporate post-open routing signals—especially escalation events. An escalation within 2 hours of opening is precisely the positive-class definition. If the score is updated in response to routing decisions that are correlated with or caused by the escalation, the score at inference time in the historical dataset would be informationally contaminated. The design document says the score is 'already produced by the support platform' but does not specify that it is snapshotted at ticket-open time. This is not a hypothetical—support platform scores are typically updated continuously and the historical value at ticket creation must be reconstructed explicitly from event logs. If it is not, this single feature could account for a large fraction of observed model improvement over the baseline, and it would not generalize in live deployment because at deployment time the score would be measured before escalation.

What would constitute evidence: (a) Verify the score is logged at ticket-open time and is immutable thereafter; (b) ablate this feature and observe whether the gap between the proposed model and baseline narrows substantially; (c) check whether the score is among the top features by permutation importance.

**Issue 2: The inter-rater filtering procedure is not a representative quality estimate, and the exclusion criterion may introduce systematic bias**

The claim: tickets with inter-rater agreement below 80% are excluded to eliminate noisy examples, and this improves training quality.

Why it may be wrong: Two mechanisms are in play. First, 500 re-labeled tickets is a small fraction of 500k+; agreement on this sample is used to validate label quality globally, but the sample may not be representative of the label noise distribution across channels, agents, or time. If label noise is clustered—e.g., one shift or one channel is systematically inconsistent—the 500-ticket sample may miss this. Second, and more importantly, the exclusion applies to tickets with low human agreement. Borderline cases—those that senior annotators themselves find ambiguous—are precisely the cases that are hardest to classify and most consequential for threshold behavior. Removing them narrows the training distribution to 'clear' cases. The model may learn a high-confidence classifier on unambiguous examples while being poorly calibrated or underperforming on the genuinely ambiguous tickets that appear in production. Since ECE is measured on test data that also excludes low-agreement tickets, the calibration estimate would not capture this gap.

What would constitute evidence: (a) Report what fraction of test-set tickets fail the 80% agreement threshold when re-labeled; (b) compare model performance on the excluded 'low-agreement' examples specifically (hold them out as a diagnostic set rather than discarding); (c) measure ECE separately on high-agreement vs. lower-agreement test examples.

**Issue 3: The baseline does not cleanly isolate historical encoding as the hypothesis mechanism**

The claim: any improvement over baseline is attributable to joint encoding of history.

Why it may be wrong: The proposed model adds three distinct capabilities over the baseline simultaneously: (a) historical text (5 prior interactions), (b) structured customer history fields, and (c) the contact-intensity score. The baseline removes all three. If the structured fields alone (loyalty tier, escalation count, disruption flag) are highly predictive—and they plausibly are, since 'escalations in past 90 days' is a direct proxy for prior urgency—then a large portion of the observed improvement may be attributable to structured features rather than historical text encoding. This matters because the architectural contribution (shared DistilBERT encoder over historical text, learned separator token) is the novel methodological claim, but no ablation distinguishes text-history contribution from structured-feature contribution. A model that beats the baseline primarily on structured features tells a different story than one that beats it on historical text.

What would constitute evidence: An intermediate ablation: logistic regression or MLP trained on current message + structured features only (no historical text). If this ablation closes most of the gap, the transformer joint-encoding contribution is modest and the methodology's claim is overstated.

**Non-issue: Threshold selection on validation set**
The methodology correctly freezes the threshold after validation and does not re-optimize on test. This is sound.

**Non-issue: Temporal split**
The temporal split is correctly motivated and correctly implemented. The concern about 'only 6 months of test data' is real but the design acknowledges it via per-month breakdowns.

**Non-issue: Vocabulary built from training set**
This is correct practice; no issue here.

**Summary:** Issue 1 (contact-intensity score leakage) is the most serious because it could silently inflate results in evaluation while failing in deployment—the canonical silent misconfiguration. Issue 2 introduces systematic bias in both training and evaluation in a correlated direction (both sets exclude ambiguous examples), making performance estimates optimistic for production. Issue 3 undermines the interpretability of the claimed mechanism even if the overall model works.
all_issues_raised: The rolling 24-hour contact-intensity score may be contaminated with post-open routing or escalation events rather than being snapshotted at ticket-open time, creating a label leakage path that would inflate evaluation metrics but fail in deployment.

The inter-rater exclusion criterion removes borderline cases from both training and test sets, potentially making calibration and F1 estimates optimistically biased for the ambiguous production cases that matter most.

The baseline removes historical text, structured features, and the contact-intensity score simultaneously, making it impossible to attribute improvement to the novel joint-encoding architecture versus simpler structured features alone.

The rolling 24-hour contact-intensity score may encode post-ticket-open contact events (computed at resolution or label-assignment time rather than ticket-open time), constituting prospective leakage into the training labels.

The 80% inter-rater agreement filter may disproportionately remove history-dependent tickets (where urgency is only apparent from context), which are precisely the cases the hypothesis is designed to address, biasing the filtered dataset against demonstrating the method's value.

The baseline conflates model class (logistic regression vs. MLP) with feature set (current-only vs. current+history), because both use DistilBERT embeddings but different downstream learners; a current-message-only transformer MLP is needed to isolate the historical feature contribution.

The validation-optimal F1 threshold may be miscalibrated for the test period if class prevalence shifts between Jan–Jun 2024 and Jul–Dec 2024 (peak summer and holiday travel), potentially distorting both absolute and relative performance estimates at test time.

The 24-hour contact-intensity feature is inherited from a production system and may encode escalation routing events that co-occur with or post-date the label-generating event, constituting label leakage that the 'no future information' claim does not verify.

The baseline conflates feature set and model class: logistic regression on current-message-only versus transformer+MLP on full features does not isolate the contribution of historical features from the contribution of nonlinear modeling capacity.

Inter-rater filtering removes ambiguous tickets from all splits, which may inflate evaluation metrics on the hard borderline cases most relevant to the business use case, and may distort temporal representativeness if ambiguous cases cluster around operational disruptions.

Why it may be wrong: The rolling 24-hour contact-intensity score 'summarizes support touches and routing events associated with the customer.' If this score is computed by the support platform at any point during the ticket's lifecycle rather than strictly at ticket-open time, it may incorporate post-open routing signals—especially escalation events. An escalation within 2 hours of opening is precisely the positive-class definition. If the score is updated in response to routing decisions that are correlated with or caused by the escalation, the score at inference time in the historical dataset would be informationally contaminated. The design document says the score is 'already produced by the support platform' but does not specify that it is snapshotted at ticket-open time. This is not a hypothetical—support platform scores are typically updated continuously and the historical value at ticket creation must be reconstructed explicitly from event logs. If it is not, this single feature could account for a large fraction of observed model improvement over the baseline, and it would not generalize in live deployment because at deployment time the score would be measured before escalation.

Why it may be wrong: Two mechanisms are in play. First, 500 re-labeled tickets is a small fraction of 500k+; agreement on this sample is used to validate label quality globally, but the sample may not be representative of the label noise distribution across channels, agents, or time. If label noise is clustered—e.g., one shift or one channel is systematically inconsistent—the 500-ticket sample may miss this. Second, and more importantly, the exclusion applies to tickets with low human agreement. Removing them narrows the training distribution to 'clear' cases. The model may learn a high-confidence classifier on unambiguous examples while being poorly calibrated or underperforming on the genuinely ambiguous tickets that appear in production. Since ECE is measured on test data that also excludes low-agreement tickets, the calibration estimate would not capture this gap.

What would constitute evidence: (a) Report what fraction of test-set tickets fail the 80% agreement threshold when re-labeled; (b) compare model performance on the excluded 'low-agreement' examples specifically (hold them out as a diagnostic set rather than discarding); (c) measure ECE separately on high-agreement vs. lower-agreement test examples.

Why it may be wrong: The proposed model adds three distinct capabilities over the baseline simultaneously: (a) historical text (5 prior interactions), (b) structured customer history fields, and (c) the contact-intensity score. The baseline removes all three. If the structured fields alone (loyalty tier, escalation count, disruption flag) are highly predictive—and they plausibly are, since 'escalations in past 90 days' is a direct proxy for prior urgency—then a large portion of the observed improvement may be attributable to structured features rather than historical text encoding. This matters because the architectural contribution (shared DistilBERT encoder over historical text, learned separator token) is the novel methodological claim, but no ablation distinguishes text-history contribution from structured-feature contribution. A model that beats the baseline primarily on structured features tells a different story than one that beats it on historical text.

What would constitute evidence: An intermediate ablation: logistic regression or MLP trained on current message + structured features only (no historical text). If this ablation closes most of the gap, the transformer joint-encoding contribution is modest and the methodology's claim is overstated.

**Non-issue: Threshold selection on validation set**
The methodology correctly freezes the threshold after validation and does not re-optimize on test. This is sound.

**Non-issue: Temporal split**
The temporal split is correctly motivated and correctly implemented. The concern about 'only 6 months of test data' is real but the design acknowledges it via per-month breakdowns.

**Non-issue: Vocabulary built from training set**
This is correct practice; no issue here.

**Summary:** Issue 1 (contact-intensity score leakage) is the most serious because it could silently inflate results in evaluation while failing in deployment—the canonical silent misconfiguration. Issue 2 introduces systematic bias in both training and evaluation in a correlated direction (both sets exclude ambiguous examples), making performance estimates optimistic for production. Issue 3 undermines the interpretability of the claimed mechanism even if the overall model works.

assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a carefully constructed methodology. The temporal split is correct, preprocessing discipline is sound, the baseline is fair (same encoder, same tuning budget, isolated to current message), and the evaluation suite is appropriate for imbalanced classification. I will work through the genuine concerns I can identify.

**Issue 1: The rolling 24-hour contact-intensity score as a near-deterministic signal**

The methodology includes a "rolling 24-hour account contact-intensity score summarizing support touches and routing events." The paper justifies this as capturing clustered-interaction urgency patterns. The specific claim is that this feature is available at prediction time and is not leaking future information.

The mechanism of potential failure: depending on how this score is computed, it may aggregate interactions *within* the same clustering event that ultimately produces the escalation. If the score counts all contact events in a 24-hour window and the model is trained on tickets that were *later* escalated (i.e., the ticket being labeled was the last in a burst that caused escalation), then the high contact-intensity score is a downstream artifact of the same cascade that defines urgency — not an independent predictor available before the label is generated. The score as described is "already produced by the support platform," which means it is a production feature, but its precise definition (rolling window anchored to what timestamp?) determines whether it constitutes prospective or retrospective information. If the score at prediction time for a ticket at T=0 includes contacts logged up to T=0 but is computed at resolution time (which may be hours later), the score used during training may encode post-opening contact bursts that would not yet be available at the moment of actual prediction in deployment.

Evidence that would resolve this: verify that the contact-intensity score feature value stored in the ticket record is always the value at ticket-open time, not the value at resolution or label-assignment time. If the system logs a snapshot at open time, this is fine. If it is computed at label time or pulled from a mutable counter, it is leakage.

**Issue 2: The inter-rater filtering procedure conflates noise with systematic disagreement**

The claim: excluding tickets with <80% inter-rater agreement removes adversarially noisy examples and produces cleaner labels.

The mechanism of potential failure: the 500 re-labeled tickets are randomly sampled, but disagreement between the external annotator and the original agent label may not be uniformly distributed across ticket types. Ambiguous tickets — where urgency is genuinely hard to assess from text alone — may systematically involve specific categories (e.g., distressed customers whose urgency is contextual and only apparent from structured history). If the 80% filtering disproportionately removes exactly the cases where history-awareness matters most (cases where urgency is not obvious from the current message alone), then the resulting dataset underrepresents the signal the hypothesis is designed to demonstrate. The model trained on filtered data may learn to classify the easy cases well while the hard, history-dependent cases have been excised. The comparison between history-aware model and baseline on the filtered dataset would then understate the lift from history, or alternatively, the test set filtered version would not contain the cases where the model is supposed to matter.

Evidence: compute the agreement rate separately for tickets where the proposed model's historical features carry high predictive weight (e.g., high recent-escalation count, active disruption flag) versus tickets where they do not. If disagreement is concentrated in history-dependent tickets, the filtering procedure is systematically removing the experiment's target population.

**Issue 3: The baseline is logistic regression, not a history-aware logistic regression or a transformer without historical features**

The stated purpose of the baseline is to isolate the contribution of history and structured features. The claim: any improvement over the logistic regression baseline is attributable to joint encoding of history.

The mechanism of potential failure: the comparison conflates two independent variables — model class (logistic regression vs. transformer MLP) and feature set (current message only vs. current + history). A transformer MLP over the same current-message-only features might outperform logistic regression on current message, meaning the observed lift is partially or entirely attributable to the richer model class rather than the historical features. The methodology acknowledges this risk implicitly ("not model class complexity") but the fix is incomplete: both models use the same DistilBERT [CLS] embedding, so the embedding capacity is matched, but the downstream learner is not (logistic regression vs. 2-layer MLP). If the transformer model with current features only (no history, no structured features) outperforms the logistic regression baseline, then the history contribution claim is confounded.

Evidence: add a third condition — the same transformer MLP architecture, trained on current message + temporal features only, no historical or structured features. If this already closes most of the gap with the full model, the history claim is weakened.

**Issue 4: Threshold selection on validation F1 interacts with class imbalance and test-set label drift**

The claim: freezing the threshold after validation prevents overfitting and provides an honest generalization estimate.

The mechanism: the optimal F1 threshold is sensitive to class prevalence. If the urgency rate in the validation set (Jan–Jun 2024) differs from the urgency rate in the test set (Jul–Dec 2024) — plausible given that Jul–Dec includes summer peak travel and holiday disruptions — then the validation-optimal threshold may be miscalibrated for the test distribution. This is not overfitting in the traditional sense, but it means test-set F1 is measured at a threshold that was optimal for a different class distribution. The reported test F1 will be lower than achievable, but more importantly, the relative comparison between model and baseline at that threshold may not reflect their true relative performance at the test distribution's optimal threshold. This is especially relevant if the two models differ in calibration (as a transformer and a logistic regression typically do).

Evidence: report test-set AUC-PR (already included) and also report the urgency rate separately for train, validation, and test periods. If the rate shifts substantially (>5 percentage points), the threshold argument needs revisiting — either by reporting threshold-free metrics (AUC-PR) as primary or by acknowledging the limitation explicitly.

**Summary:**

Issue 1 (contact-intensity score timestamp) is potentially a genuine leakage issue depending on implementation — it is not certain to be a flaw, but the methodology does not provide evidence it is safe. Issue 3 (baseline conflation of model class and feature set) is a real gap in the experimental design that prevents the methodology from cleanly attributing the claimed lift to historical features. Issues 2 and 4 are real but less severe — they would attenuate effect sizes rather than invalidate the comparison.

Issue 3 is the strongest structural flaw: the experiment as described cannot definitively attribute observed improvement to history-awareness versus model capacity.

issues_raised: The 24-hour contact-intensity feature is inherited from a production system and may encode escalation routing events that co-occur with or post-date the label-generating event, constituting label leakage that the 'no future information' claim does not verify.

The baseline conflates feature set and model class: logistic regression on current-message-only versus transformer+MLP on full features does not isolate the contribution of historical features from the contribution of nonlinear modeling capacity.

Inter-rater filtering removes ambiguous tickets from all splits, which may inflate evaluation metrics on the hard borderline cases most relevant to the business use case, and may distort temporal representativeness if ambiguous cases cluster around operational disruptions.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
The methodology is thoughtfully designed in many respects — temporal splitting, single test-set evaluation, preprocessing fitted on training only, and explicit exclusion of future information are all correct. However, I identify three issues worth naming precisely.

**Issue 1: The 24-hour contact-intensity score is a potential leakage vector for the label definition**

The label is defined as escalation within 2 hours of ticket opening, or priority ≥3. The feature set includes a 'rolling 24-hour account contact-intensity score summarizing support touches and routing events.' If this score is computed over a window that includes the current ticket's own routing events or touches that co-occur with or post-date the escalation decision, it encodes the label-generating process rather than a predictor of it. The design says the score is 'already produced by the support platform,' which means its exact computation window is inherited from an operational system, not designed for this experiment. If that system updates the score in response to escalation routing (a common pattern in production support infrastructure where escalation is itself a routing event), then including this feature for tickets where escalation has already occurred constitutes direct label leakage. This is not hypothetical: in real support platforms, the contact-intensity signal and the escalation trigger are often produced by the same event stream. The authors claim to 'explicitly exclude future information' but do not verify this holds for the contact-intensity feature specifically.

Evidence that would resolve this: Check whether the contact-intensity score value differs systematically between urgent and non-urgent tickets in a way that is not explained by prior-interaction clustering alone. More directly, compare model performance with and without this feature. A large drop in AUC when this single feature is removed would be a red flag. The correct fix is to ensure the score is computed using only events strictly prior to ticket-open timestamp.

**Issue 2: The baseline is not a fair ablation of history — it is also an ablation of model capacity**

The proposed model is a transformer joint encoder with a 2-layer MLP head. The baseline is logistic regression on only the current-message [CLS] embedding. The authors claim 'any improvement is attributable to joint encoding, not model class complexity.' This is wrong. Logistic regression is a linear model over a fixed embedding; the proposed model applies a nonlinear MLP over a richer feature set. These two things co-vary: if the proposed model wins, it could be because of history features, or because of the MLP's nonlinearity applied to the current-message embedding alone, or both. The baseline does not isolate the contribution of history. A proper ablation would use the same architecture (DistilBERT + 2-layer MLP) trained on current-message-only features. The current comparison conflates the modeling advantage with the feature advantage.

Evidence that would resolve this: Add an intermediate ablation — same transformer + MLP architecture, but with historical features removed. If the proposed model beats this intermediate ablation, history is responsible. If the proposed model only beats the logistic regression baseline, the conclusion is confounded.

**Issue 3: The inter-rater filtering procedure is poorly specified as a data quality mechanism and may introduce selection bias**

The methodology filters out tickets where external annotator agreement with the original agent label is below 80%. The intent is to remove noisy labels. But the mechanism has two problems. First, the external annotator is labeling retrospectively with full ticket context, while the agent label was produced in real time under operational pressure. Disagreement may indicate genuine label ambiguity (which is exactly the hard case the model needs to learn from) rather than noise. Filtering these cases systematically removes difficult borderline examples, potentially inflating both train-time performance and evaluation metrics — the model never sees the cases where urgency is genuinely ambiguous. Second, the filtering is applied across all splits. If ambiguous tickets cluster in particular time windows (e.g., during operational disruptions when urgency criteria are contested), removing them could undermine the temporal representativeness of the test set — precisely the evaluation the temporal split was designed to protect. The methodology acknowledges label noise as a risk but the proposed remedy may worsen distributional distortion rather than reduce it.

Evidence that would resolve this: Report the rate of excluded tickets by time period, channel, and customer tier. If exclusions cluster in specific strata, those strata's test performance will be overestimated. Additionally, compare model performance on retained vs. excluded tickets if feasible (by using excluded tickets as a separate held-out set with the annotator's labels as ground truth).

**Summary:** Issue 1 is the most serious because it is a potential leakage from an inherited production feature whose computation semantics are not verified. Issue 2 is a straightforward design error in the ablation. Issue 3 is a secondary concern but could cause the evaluation to systematically over-report performance on the hard cases that matter most operationally.
